lessons.title: My Family
lessons.description: Reading comprehension: a simple family portrait using A2 present tense

readingPassage.context: Family and hospitality sit at the center of daily life across the Arabic-speaking world — offering a guest food or coffee within minutes of arrival is an expected courtesy, and multi-generational households remain far more common than in many Western countries.

paragraphs.ar: اسمي سارة، وعندي عائلة كبيرة وسعيدة.
paragraphs.en: My name is Sarah, and I have a big, happy family.

paragraphs.ar: أبي مهندسٌ، وهو يعمل في شركة كبيرة في المدينة.
paragraphs.en: My father is an engineer, and he works at a large company in the city.

paragraphs.ar: أمي طبيبةٌ، وهي تعمل في مستشفى قريب من بيتنا.
paragraphs.en: My mother is a doctor, and she works at a hospital near our house.

paragraphs.ar: عندي أخٌ وأختٌ. أخي يدرس الطب، وأختي تدرس الهندسة.
paragraphs.en: I have a brother and a sister. My brother studies medicine, and my sister studies engineering.

paragraphs.ar: نحن نسكن في بيت كبير وجميل، وحديقتنا مليئة بالأزهار.
paragraphs.en: We live in a big, beautiful house, and our garden is full of flowers.

paragraphs.ar: كل يوم أحد، نذهب معًا إلى بيت جدي وجدتي.
paragraphs.en: Every Sunday, we go together to my grandfather and grandmother's house.

paragraphs.ar: جدي يحكي لنا قصصًا قديمة، وجدتي تطبخ لنا أطباقًا لذيذة.
paragraphs.en: My grandfather tells us old stories, and my grandmother cooks us delicious dishes.

paragraphs.ar: أحب عائلتي كثيرًا، وأنا سعيدة جدًّا بها.

lessons.title: My New Job
lessons.description: Reading comprehension: iḍāfa and future tense in a workplace narrative

readingPassage.context: Software and tech companies have boomed across Cairo, Amman, and the Gulf over the last decade, creating a new professional vocabulary that blends Arabic grammar with borrowed English terms like سوفتوير and ستارت أب.

paragraphs.ar: اسمي مروان، وبدأتُ وظيفتي الجديدة في شركةِ برمجياتٍ الأسبوعَ الماضي.
paragraphs.en: My name is Marwan, and I started my new job at a software company last week.

paragraphs.ar: مكتبي في الطابقِ العاشرِ، وله إطلالةٌ جميلةٌ على وسطِ المدينةِ.
paragraphs.en: My office is on the tenth floor, and it has a beautiful view of downtown.

paragraphs.ar: مديرُ القسمِ رجلٌ لطيفٌ، وهو يساعد الموظفين الجدد كثيرًا.
paragraphs.en: The department manager is a kind man, and he helps new employees a lot.

paragraphs.ar: سأتعلّم مهاراتٍ جديدةً في الأشهر القادمة، وسوف أحضر دوراتٍ تدريبيةً كل شهر.
paragraphs.en: I will learn new skills in the coming months, and I will attend training courses every month.

paragraphs.ar: زملائي في العمل ودودون، وقد دعوني لتناول الغداء معهم في أول يوم.
paragraphs.en: My colleagues at work are friendly, and they invited me to have lunch with them on the first day.

paragraphs.ar: الأسبوع القادم، سأقدّم أول تقريرٍ لي أمام فريق الإدارة.
paragraphs.en: Next week, I will present my first report in front of the management team.

paragraphs.ar: أعرف أن بداية أي وظيفةٍ جديدةٍ صعبةٌ، لكنني متحمسٌ لهذه الفرصة.
paragraphs.en: I know the beginning of any new job is difficult, but I'm excited about this opportunity.

exercises.question: What kind of company does Marwan work at?
options: A bank

A restaurant

exercises.question: What view does his office have?
options: The sea
The mountains
Downtown

exercises.question: What did his colleagues do on his first day?
options: Ignored him

exercises.question: How does Marwan feel about the new job?
options: Regretful

Afraid of being fired
Excited despite the difficulty

lessons.title: Cairo or Dubai?
lessons.description: Reading comprehension: comparatives and superlatives comparing two great cities

readingPassage.context: Cairo's Islamic quarter and the Giza pyramids are UNESCO World Heritage sites dating back over 4,500 years, while Dubai grew from a small pearling and fishing village into a global city within a single human lifetime.

paragraphs.ar: القاهرة ودبي مدينتان عظيمتان، لكنهما مختلفتان جدًّا.
paragraphs.en: Cairo and Dubai are two great cities, but they are very different.

paragraphs.ar: القاهرة أقدم من دبي بآلاف السنين؛ فتاريخها يعود إلى العصور الفرعونية.
paragraphs.en: Cairo is older than Dubai by thousands of years; its history goes back to the Pharaonic era.

paragraphs.ar: أما دبي، فهي أحدث بكثير، لكنها نمت بسرعةٍ أكبر من أي مدينةٍ أخرى في المنطقة.
paragraphs.en: As for Dubai, it's much newer, but it grew faster than any other city in the region.

paragraphs.ar: برجُ خليفةَ في دبي هو أطولُ مبنًى في العالم، بينما تشتهر القاهرة بأهراماتها القديمة.
paragraphs.en: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai is the tallest building in the world, while Cairo is famous for its ancient pyramids.

paragraphs.ar: عدد سكان القاهرة أكبر بكثير من عدد سكان دبي.
paragraphs.en: Cairo's population is much larger than Dubai's population.

paragraphs.ar: لكن دبي أغلى من القاهرة في تكاليف المعيشة بشكل عام.
paragraphs.en: But Dubai is more expensive than Cairo in terms of general living costs.

paragraphs.ar: كلتا المدينتين هي الأهم اقتصاديًّا في منطقتها.
paragraphs.en: Both cities are the most important economically in their region.

paragraphs.ar: وفي رأيي، كل مدينةٍ لها سحرها الخاص، ولا يمكن مقارنة إحداهما بالأخرى بسهولة.
paragraphs.en: In my opinion, each city has its own charm, and one cannot be easily compared to the other.

lessons.title: A Day in a Student's Life
lessons.description: Reading comprehension: nine linked sentences combining everything from A2–B2

paragraphs.ar: اسْمِي أَحْمَدُ، وَأَنَا طَالِبٌ فِي جَامِعَةٍ كَبِيرَةٍ فِي الْقَاهِرَةِ.
paragraphs.en: My name is Ahmad, and I am a student at a big university in Cairo.

paragraphs.ar: أَذْهَبُ إِلَى الْجَامِعَةِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ فِي السَّاعَةِ السَّابِعَةِ صَبَاحًا.
paragraphs.en: I go to university every day at seven in the morning.

paragraphs.ar: أَمْسِ، ذَهَبْتُ إِلَى الْمَكْتَبَةِ الَّتِي تَقَعُ بِجَانِبِ كُلِّيَّتِي.
paragraphs.en: Yesterday, I went to the library that is located next to my college.

paragraphs.ar: قَرَأْتُ كِتَابًا عَنِ التَّارِيخِ الْإِسْلَامِيِّ، وَفَهِمْتُ الْكَثِيرَ مِنَ الْأَحْدَاثِ الْمُهِمَّةِ.
paragraphs.en: I read a book about Islamic history, and I understood many of the important events.

paragraphs.ar: الْأُسْتَاذُ الَّذِي يُدَرِّسُ هَذِهِ الْمَادَّةَ رَجُلٌ عَالِمٌ وَصَبُورٌ.
paragraphs.en: The professor who teaches this subject is a knowledgeable and patient man.

paragraphs.ar: لَنْ أَنْسَى الدُّرُوسَ الَّتِي تَعَلَّمْتُهَا مِنْهُ.
paragraphs.en: I will never forget the lessons that I learned from him.

paragraphs.ar: غَدًا، سَأَذْهَبُ إِلَى السُّوقِ لِأَشْتَرِيَ كِتَابًا جَدِيدًا.
paragraphs.en: Tomorrow, I will go to the market to buy a new book.

paragraphs.ar: لَا أُحِبُّ أَنْ أُضَيِّعَ وَقْتِي، لِأَنَّ الْوَقْتَ كَالسَّيْفِ.
paragraphs.en: I don't like to waste my time, because time is like a sword.

paragraphs.ar: أَتَمَنَّى أَنْ أَنْجَحَ فِي دِرَاسَتِي وَأَخْدُمَ بَلَدِي فِي الْمُسْتَقْبَلِ.
paragraphs.en: I hope to succeed in my studies and serve my country in the future.

readingPassage.context: Cairo is home to Al-Azhar University, founded in 970 CE — one of the oldest continuously operating universities in the world — as well as Cairo University, a major educational hub for students across the Arab world.

lessons.title: Advice for Success
lessons.description: Reading comprehension: a chain of real conditionals in a self-help register

readingPassage.context: Arabic-language self-improvement content is one of the fastest-growing genres on social media across the Arab world, often blending older wisdom traditions — proverbs, religious sayings — with contemporary self-help psychology.

paragraphs.ar: يسألني كثيرٌ من الشباب: كيف أنجح في حياتي؟ وإليكم بعض النصائح البسيطة.
paragraphs.en: Many young people ask me: how do I succeed in my life? Here are some simple pieces of advice.

paragraphs.ar: إذا حدّدتَ هدفًا واضحًا، ستعرف إلى أين تتجه.
paragraphs.en: If you set a clear goal, you'll know where you're headed.

paragraphs.ar: وإذا خطّطتَ يومكَ مسبقًا، ستستخدم وقتكَ بشكلٍ أفضل.
paragraphs.en: And if you plan your day in advance, you'll use your time better.

paragraphs.ar: إذا واجهتَ فشلًا، فلا تستسلم؛ فالفشل جزءٌ طبيعيٌّ من النجاح.
paragraphs.en: If you face failure, don't give up; failure is a natural part of success.

paragraphs.ar: وإذا طلبتَ المساعدة عندما تحتاجها، فلن تشعر بالوحدة في رحلتك.
paragraphs.en: And if you ask for help when you need it, you won't feel alone on your journey.

paragraphs.ar: إذا آمنتَ بنفسكَ، سيؤمن الآخرون بكَ أيضًا.
paragraphs.en: If you believe in yourself, others will believe in you too.

paragraphs.ar: وأخيرًا، إذا استمررتَ رغم الصعوبات، فستصل حتمًا إلى ما تريد.
paragraphs.en: And finally, if you keep going despite the difficulties, you will surely reach what you want.

exercises.question: According to the text, what happens if you set a clear goal?
options: You'll succeed immediately
You'll know where you're headed
You'll avoid all failure
Nothing changes

exercises.question: What should you do if you face failure?
options: Quit immediately
Blame others
Not give up — it's natural
Hide it from others

exercises.question: What happens if you ask for help when you need it?
options: You'll seem weak
You won't feel alone on your journey
You'll fail faster
Nothing happens

exercises.question: What's the relationship between self-belief and others' belief in you?

lessons.title: Reading: On Wasting Time
lessons.description: An op-ed style passage in undiacritized MSA — the register of real advanced text

paragraphs.ar: يقول كثير من الناس إنهم لا يجدون وقتاً كافياً لإنجاز أعمالهم، لكن المشكلة الحقيقية ليست في نقص الوقت بل في سوء إدارته.
paragraphs.en: Many people say they don't have enough time to get their work done, but the real problem isn't a lack of time — it's mismanaging it.

paragraphs.ar: فالإنسان الذي يؤجل مهامه إلى اللحظة الأخيرة غالباً ما يجد نفسه غارقاً في التوتر والقلق.
paragraphs.en: A person who puts off tasks until the last moment often finds himself drowning in stress and anxiety.

paragraphs.ar: يذكّرنا المثل العربي بأن الوقت كالسيف، إن لم تقطعه قطعك.
paragraphs.en: The Arabic proverb reminds us: time is like a sword — if you don't cut it, it cuts you.

paragraphs.ar: ومن هنا تنبع أهمية التخطيط اليومي، إذ إن تحديد الأولويات يساعد على إنجاز الأهم قبل المهم.
paragraphs.en: This is where the importance of daily planning comes from, since setting priorities helps you get the most important things done before the merely important ones.

paragraphs.ar: أظهرت دراسات حديثة أن الأشخاص الذين يخصصون وقتاً للراحة بين المهام ينجزون عملاً أكثر بجودة أعلى.
paragraphs.en: Recent studies have shown that people who set aside time to rest between tasks accomplish more, at higher quality.

paragraphs.ar: ورغم ذلك، ما زال كثيرون يعتقدون أن الانشغال الدائم دليل على النجاح، وهذا اعتقاد خاطئ في كثير من الأحيان.
paragraphs.en: And yet many still believe constant busyness is proof of success — a belief that is mistaken more often than not.

paragraphs.ar: لذلك، فإن إدارة الوقت ليست ترفاً بل ضرورة لكل من يسعى إلى تحقيق أهدافه دون أن يفقد توازنه.
paragraphs.en: Time management, then, is not a luxury but a necessity for anyone trying to reach their goals without losing their balance.

readingPassage.context: The line "time is like a sword" is a well-known Arabic proverb often invoked in exactly this kind of productivity writing — a reminder that the self-help genre has deep roots in older Arabic literary and religious tradition, not just modern psychology.

exercises.question: How does the final sentence characterize time management?
options: A luxury for the wealthy

A necessity, not a luxury
Something only managers need

lessons.title: The Story of the Arabic Language
lessons.description: Reading comprehension: a closing passage about Arabic itself, in undiacritized advanced register

readingPassage.context: A fitting final text for this course — tying together the root system from Lesson 1, the iḍāfa chains from B1, and the counterfactual لو you just practiced, all in service of the language's own story.

paragraphs.ar: تُعَدُّ اللغةُ العربيةُ واحدةً من أقدم اللغات الحية في العالم، إذ يعود تاريخ الشعر العربي المدوَّن إلى ما قبل الإسلام بقرون.
paragraphs.en: Arabic is considered one of the oldest living languages in the world, since recorded Arabic poetry dates back centuries before Islam.

paragraphs.ar: ولولا نزول القرآن الكريم بالعربية في القرن السابع الميلادي، لما اتخذت اللغة هذا الشكل الموحّد الذي نعرفه اليوم كفصحى.
paragraphs.en: Were it not for the revelation of the Quran in Arabic in the seventh century CE, the language would not have taken the unified form we know today as Fusha.

paragraphs.ar: وعندما انتشر الإسلام من الأندلس غربًا إلى حدود الصين شرقًا، انتشرت العربية معه لغةَ علمٍ وإدارةٍ وتجارة.
paragraphs.en: And when Islam spread from Andalusia in the west to the borders of China in the east, Arabic spread with it as a language of science, administration, and trade.

paragraphs.ar: ومهما اختلفت اللهجاتُ المحكية من المغرب إلى الخليج، تبقى الفصحى هي الرابط المشترك بين جميع المتحدثين بالعربية.
paragraphs.en: However much the spoken dialects differ from Morocco to the Gulf, Fusha remains the common thread linking all Arabic speakers.

paragraphs.ar: تُبنى معظم الكلمات العربية من جذرٍ ثلاثي الحروف، وهذا النظام يتيح توليد المئات من الكلمات من جذرٍ واحدٍ فقط.
paragraphs.en: Most Arabic words are built from a three-letter root, and this system allows for generating hundreds of words from just a single root.

paragraphs.ar: اليوم، تُعَدُّ العربية إحدى اللغات الرسمية الست في الأمم المتحدة، ويتحدث بها أكثر من أربعمئة مليون إنسانٍ حول العالم.
paragraphs.en: Today, Arabic is one of the six official languages of the United Nations, and more than four hundred million people around the world speak it.

paragraphs.ar: ولو لم تحافظ الفصحى على هذا الشكل الموحّد عبر القرون، لكانت العربية قد انقسمت اليوم إلى لغاتٍ منفصلةً، كما حدث مع اللاتينية.
paragraphs.en: And if Fusha had not preserved this unified form across the centuries, Arabic would have split into separate languages today, as happened with Latin.

exercises.question: What does the passage say gave Fusha its unified form?
options: Modern schooling systems
The revelation of the Quran in the 7th century
Colonial administration
The printing press

exercises.question: How far did Arabic spread along with Islam, per the passage?
options: Only within the Arabian peninsula
From Andalusia to the borders of China
To Europe only
Worldwide immediately

exercises.question: What does the three-letter root system allow?
options: Makes the language harder to learn
Is unique to Arabic among world languages
Generating hundreds of words from one root
Was invented in modern times

exercises.question: How many UN official languages include Arabic, and how many speakers does it have?
options: The only official language; under 50 million speakers
One of six; over 400 million speakers
One of two; about 100 million speakers
Not an official UN language

exercises.question: What does the passage compare Arabic's possible fate to, had Fusha not stayed unified?
options: Greek disappearing entirely
Latin splitting into separate languages
English absorbing other languages
Chinese dialectal split

a2-present-verbs.title: The Present Tense (المضارع)
a2-present-verbs.pattern: أَ / تَ / يَ / نَ + verb stem
a2-present-verbs.explanation: Arabic present-tense verbs don't use a separate pronoun the way English does — the person is built into a prefix (and sometimes a suffix) on the verb itself. أنا أذهب already means 'I go'; adding أنا is optional emphasis, not a requirement. Each person gets its own prefix: أ- for أنا, تـ- for أنتَ/هي, يـ- for هو, نـ- for نحن, and تـ...ين / يـ...ون add suffixes for أنتِ and هم.
a2-present-verbs.fact: Arabic verbs are built from a three-consonant root (like ذ-ه-ب, 'go') that carries the core meaning, while vowels and prefixes around it carry the grammar. The same root ذهب gives you ذهب (he went), يذهب (he goes), ذاهب (going), مذهب (a school of thought — literally 'a place one goes to'). Learn the root, and a whole constellation of words opens up.

dialogue.ar: أينَ تَعْمَلُ؟
dialogue.en: Where do you work?

dialogue.ar: أَعْمَلُ فِي مَكْتَبٍ كَبِيرٍ فِي وَسَطِ الْمَدِينَةِ.
dialogue.en: I work in a big office downtown.

dialogue.ar: وَهَلْ تَذْهَبُ إِلَى هُنَاكَ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ؟
dialogue.en: And do you go there every day?

dialogue.ar: نَعَمْ، أَذْهَبُ كُلَّ صَبَاحٍ بِالْحَافِلَةِ.
dialogue.en: Yes, I go every morning by bus.

a2-adjectives.title: Adjective Agreement (النعت)
a2-adjectives.explanation: Arabic adjectives follow the noun and must agree with it in definiteness, gender, and number: البيتُ كبيرٌ (the house is big, both indefinite-marked/nakira in case ending) vs البيتُ الكبيرُ (the big house, both definite with ال). Feminine nouns almost always end in ة, and their adjectives pick up a matching ة: مدينة كبيرة, not مدينة كبير.
a2-adjectives.fact: Arabic has no separate word for 'is' in simple present-tense descriptions — البيتُ كبيرٌ is literally 'the-house big', with no verb at all. Linguists call this a 'nominal sentence' (جملة اسمية). The verb 'to be' only shows up in the past (كان) or future (سيكون), or for emphasis.

dialogue.ar: كَيْفَ بَيْتُكُمُ الْجَدِيدُ؟
dialogue.en: How is your new house?

dialogue.ar: بَيْتُنَا كَبِيرٌ وَجَمِيلٌ، وَالْحَيُّ هَادِئٌ جِدًّا.
dialogue.en: Our house is big and beautiful, and the neighborhood is very quiet.

dialogue.ar: رَائِعٌ! وَهَلِ الْمَدْرَسَةُ قَرِيبَةٌ؟
dialogue.en: Wonderful! And is the school nearby?

a2-demonstratives.title: This, That, and عند (Having)
a2-demonstratives.explanation: هذا is 'this' for masculine nouns, هذه for feminine. ذلك and تلك work the same way for 'that'. Arabic has no verb 'to have' — instead it uses the preposition عند ('at/by') with a possessive pronoun suffix: عندي كتابٌ literally means 'at-me [is] a book', i.e. 'I have a book'.
a2-demonstratives.fact: This عند construction is shared with Hebrew and other Semitic languages, which also lack a true verb 'to have'. It reflects a broader Semitic pattern of expressing possession through location ('with me is X') rather than an action ('I hold X').

dialogue.ar: هَلْ عِنْدَكَ قَلَمٌ؟
dialogue.en: Do you have a pen?

dialogue.ar: نَعَمْ، عِنْدِي قَلَمٌ جَدِيدٌ. تَفَضَّلِي.
dialogue.en: Yes, I have a new pen. Here you go.

dialogue.ar: شُكْرًا. وَمَا هَذَا الْكِتَابُ؟
dialogue.en: Thanks. And what's this book?

dialogue.ar: هَذَا كِتَابِي فِي التَّارِيخِ.
dialogue.en: This is my history book.

a2-past-verbs.title: The Past Tense (الماضي)
a2-past-verbs.pattern: verb stem + تُ / تَ / تِ / (nothing) / تْ / نَا / وا
a2-past-verbs.explanation: The past tense adds a suffix to the verb stem instead of a prefix: ذهبتُ (I went), ذهبتَ (you, m., went), ذهبتِ (you, f., went), ذهبَ (he went), ذهبتْ (she went), ذهبنا (we went), ذهبوا (they went). Notice the stem ذهب never changes — only the ending does.
a2-past-verbs.fact: Because the past tense is built by adding endings to a fixed stem, and the present tense by adding prefixes, a fluent reader can often identify a verb's tense at a glance just from its silhouette — prefix-heavy words lean present, suffix-heavy words lean past.

dialogue.ar: أَيْنَ ذَهَبْتَ أَمْسِ؟
dialogue.en: Where did you go yesterday?

dialogue.ar: ذَهَبْتُ إِلَى السُّوقِ وَاشْتَرَيْتُ بَعْضَ الْكُتُبِ.
dialogue.en: I went to the market and bought some books.

dialogue.ar: وَهَلْ قَرَأْتَ أَيًّا مِنْهَا؟
dialogue.en: And did you read any of them?

dialogue.ar: قَرَأْتُ وَاحِدًا فَقَطْ حَتَّى الْآنَ.
dialogue.en: I've only read one so far.

a2-past-negation.title: Negating and Questioning the Past
a2-past-negation.pattern: لَمْ + jussive present (= simple past negative) · هل / متى / أين + past verb
a2-past-negation.explanation: To negate a past action, Standard Arabic usually doesn't negate the past-tense verb directly — instead it uses لم ('did not') followed by the present-tense verb in the jussive mood: لم أذهب ('I did not go', literally 'not I-go [jussive]'). Questions about the past use the same question words as elsewhere — هل for yes/no, متى for when, أين for where — placed before the verb.
a2-past-negation.fact: This لم + jussive pattern is one of the more surprising things for new learners: the 'past negative' grammatically borrows the present-tense verb form. It's a reminder that Arabic tense and mood are separate systems that interact in ways that don't map cleanly onto English tenses.

dialogue.ar: هَلْ ذَهَبْتَ إِلَى الْعَمَلِ أَمْسِ؟
dialogue.en: Did you go to work yesterday?

dialogue.ar: لَا، لَمْ أَذْهَبْ. كُنْتُ مَرِيضَةً.
dialogue.en: No, I didn't go. I was sick.

dialogue.ar: مَتَى مَرِضْتِ؟
dialogue.en: When did you get sick?

dialogue.ar: مُنْذُ يَوْمَيْنِ فَقَطْ.
dialogue.en: Just two days ago.

a2-kaana-temporal.title: كان (Was) and Temporal Clauses
a2-kaana-temporal.pattern: كان + noun/adjective = 'was' · قبل أن / بعد أن = before / after
a2-kaana-temporal.explanation: كان is the past-tense verb 'to be', used where a nominal sentence would otherwise have no verb at all: الجوُّ جميلٌ (the weather is beautiful) becomes كان الجوُّ جميلًا (the weather was beautiful) — note the adjective switches to the accusative case. قبل أن and بعد أن ('before' and 'after') introduce a full clause with its own verb.
a2-kaana-temporal.fact: كان belongs to a special class Arabic grammarians call 'sisters of كان' (أخوات كان) — a family of about a dozen verbs (أصبح 'became', ظلّ 'remained', ما زال 'is still') that all share this same grammatical behavior of putting the following adjective into the accusative case.

dialogue.ar: كَيْفَ كَانَتِ الرِّحْلَةُ؟
dialogue.en: How was the trip?

dialogue.ar: كَانَتْ طَوِيلَةً، لَكِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مُمْتِعَةً جِدًّا.
dialogue.en: It was long, but it was very enjoyable.

dialogue.ar: مَاذَا فَعَلْتُمْ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَعُودُوا؟
dialogue.en: What did you do before you came back?

b1-idafa-simple.title: Possession: Iḍāfa (الإضافة)
b1-idafa-simple.pattern: noun₁ (no ال, no tanwīn) + noun₂ (definite) → 'the noun₁ of noun₂'
b1-idafa-simple.explanation: Where English uses 's or 'of', Arabic simply places two nouns side by side: بابُ البيتِ ('the door of the house'). The first noun drops both the definite article ال and the indefinite ending (tanwīn) — it borrows its definiteness from the second noun. Add a third noun and you get a chain: مفتاحُ بابِ البيتِ ('the key of the door of the house').
b1-idafa-simple.fact: Iḍāfa literally means 'addition' or 'annexation' in Arabic grammar. It's one of the most productive structures in the language — entire institution names are built this way, like وزارة الخارجية ('the ministry of foreign affairs', literally 'the ministry of the foreign-ness').

dialogue.ar: أَيْنَ مِفْتَاحُ السَّيَّارَةِ؟
dialogue.en: Where's the car key?

dialogue.ar: عَلَى طَاوِلَةِ الْمَطْبَخِ.
dialogue.en: On the kitchen table.

dialogue.ar: شُكْرًا، وَأَيْنَ حَقِيبَةُ أُخْتِي؟
dialogue.en: Thanks, and where's my sister's bag?

b1-idafa-abstract.title: Abstract Iḍāfa and Superlatives
b1-idafa-abstract.pattern: abstract noun + noun (iḍāfa) · أفعل + noun (definite, singular) = superlative iḍāfa
b1-idafa-abstract.explanation: Iḍāfa isn't limited to physical possession — it works just as well for abstract relationships: معنى الكلمةِ ('the meaning of the word'), مستوى الطالبِ ('the student's level'). Superlatives use the same mechanism: أجملُ مدينةٍ ('the most beautiful city') puts the elative (أفعل) form first, annexed to an indefinite singular noun.
b1-idafa-abstract.fact: The superlative-as-iḍāfa pattern (أفضل صديقٍ لي, 'my best friend') is grammatically the same structure as بابُ البيتِ — Arabic doesn't distinguish 'the door of the house' from 'the best of friends' syntactically. Both are just noun + noun.

dialogue.ar: مَا مَعْنَى هَذِهِ الْكَلِمَةِ؟
dialogue.en: What's the meaning of this word?

dialogue.ar: هَذَا سُؤَالٌ جَيِّدٌ. جُذُورُهَا عَمِيقَةٌ فِي التَّارِيخِ.
dialogue.en: That's a good question. Its roots run deep in history.

dialogue.ar: وَمَا أَفْضَلُ طَرِيقَةٍ لِتَعَلُّمِهَا؟
dialogue.en: And what's the best way to learn it?

b1-future.title: The Future Tense (المستقبل)
b1-future.pattern: سَ + present verb (near future) · سوف + present verb (general future)
b1-future.explanation: Arabic builds the future by attaching سَـ directly onto the present-tense verb (سأذهب, 'I will go') or, more formally, by placing the free-standing particle سوف in front of it (سوف أذهب). Both mean the same thing; سـ is more common in everyday speech, سوف leans slightly more formal or emphatic.
b1-future.fact: سوف is thought to come from the same root as a word meaning 'soon' or 'presently' — its grammaticalization into a pure future marker happened gradually, the same way English 'going to' slid from literal motion into a future auxiliary ('I'm gonna go').

dialogue.ar: مَاذَا سَتَفْعَلُ غَدًا؟
dialogue.en: What will you do tomorrow?

dialogue.ar: سَأَذْهَبُ إِلَى السُّوقِ، ثُمَّ سَوْفَ أَزُورُ جَدَّتِي.
dialogue.en: I'll go to the market, then I'll visit my grandmother.

dialogue.ar: هَلْ سَتَعُودِينَ مُتَأَخِّرَةً؟
dialogue.en: Will you come back late?

b1-negation.title: Negation: لن, لا, and Prohibition
b1-negation.pattern: لن + subjunctive = won't · لا + present = don't (habit) · لا + jussive = don't! (command)
b1-negation.explanation: Arabic has a different negation particle for almost every tense. لن negates the future and forces the following verb into the subjunctive mood (لن أذهبَ, 'I will not go'). لا with a plain present-tense verb negates habits or general truths (لا أشربُ القهوة, 'I don't drink coffee'). لا with the jussive mood forms a direct prohibition — a command not to do something (لا تذهبْ, 'don't go!').
b1-negation.fact: That three-way split — a dedicated particle for future negation, a separate one for habitual negation, and a mood-shift for commands — has no single English equivalent; English just uses 'not' or 'don't' for all three. It's a small window into how Arabic grammar encodes time and intention more explicitly than English does.

dialogue.ar: لَنْ أَذْهَبَ إِلَى الْحَفْلَةِ اللَّيْلَةَ.
dialogue.en: I won't go to the party tonight.

dialogue.ar: لِمَاذَا؟ أَنْتَ لَا تَخْرُجُ أَبَدًا!
dialogue.en: Why? You never go out!

dialogue.ar: لَا تَقْلَقِي، سَأَخْرُجُ فِي عُطْلَةِ الْأُسْبُوعِ.
dialogue.en: Don't worry, I'll go out on the weekend.

b1-hedging.title: Opinion Verbs + أنّ
b1-hedging.pattern: أظنّ / أعتقد / أخشى + أنّ + [subject in accusative] + [clause]
b1-hedging.explanation: Verbs of opinion, belief, and fear — أظن (I think), أعتقد (I believe), أخشى (I fear), من المحتمل (it's likely) — introduce a clause with أنّ ('that'), and the subject of that clause takes the accusative case: أظنّ أنّه سينجحُ ('I think that he will succeed' — أنّه = أنّ + ـه, 'him/he' in the accusative).
b1-hedging.fact: أنّ and its shorter cousin أنْ look almost identical but do very different jobs: أنّ introduces a factual statement (with an accusative subject), while أنْ introduces a subjunctive verb of intention or possibility (أريد أن أذهب, 'I want to go'). Mixing them up is one of the most common mistakes even advanced learners make.

dialogue.ar: هَلْ تَظُنُّ أَنَّهُ سَيَنْجَحُ فِي الِامْتِحَانِ؟
dialogue.en: Do you think he'll pass the exam?

dialogue.ar: أَعْتَقِدُ ذَلِكَ، لَكِنِّي أَخْشَى أَنْ يَكُونَ مُتَوَتِّرًا.
dialogue.en: I believe so, but I'm afraid he might be nervous.

b2-relative.title: Relative Clauses: الذي / التي / الذين
b2-relative.pattern: definite noun + الذي (m.) / التي (f.) / الذين (pl.) + clause with a resumptive pronoun
b2-relative.explanation: To say 'the book that I read', Arabic uses الذي only after a definite noun, and — crucially — the clause that follows needs its own pronoun referring back to the noun: الكتابُ الذي قرأتُهُ ('the book that I-read-it'). That extra ـهُ ('it') is called a resumptive pronoun, and leaving it out is a very common learner mistake.
b2-relative.fact: If the noun being described is indefinite, الذي disappears entirely and the resumptive pronoun does all the work on its own: كتابٌ قرأتُهُ ('a book I read it' = 'a book that I read'). The relative pronoun is only needed to point back to something definite.

dialogue.ar: هَلْ قَرَأْتَ الْكِتَابَ الَّذِي أَعْطَيْتُكَ إِيَّاهُ؟
dialogue.en: Did you read the book I gave you?

dialogue.ar: نَعَمْ، وَالشَّخْصِيَّةُ الَّتِي أَحْبَبْتُهَا أَكْثَرَ هِيَ الْبَطَلُ.
dialogue.en: Yes, and the character I liked most was the hero.

b2-indefinite-relative.title: Whoever, Whatever: مَن / مَا
b2-indefinite-relative.explanation: من and ما work like الذي but need no antecedent noun at all — they mean 'whoever' and 'whatever' by themselves: من يجتهدْ ينجحْ ('whoever works hard succeeds'). This is the structure behind many Arabic proverbs, where the compact, noun-free phrasing gives the saying its punch.
b2-indefinite-relative.fact: من in this 'whoever' sense is grammatically a conditional word, which is why the verbs after it often appear in the jussive mood (as if it were an إذا/إنْ sentence) — من يزرعْ يحصدْ ('whoever plants, harvests') behaves exactly like إن تزرعْ تحصدْ grammatically.

dialogue.ar: مَنْ يَجِدُّ يَجِدْ يَا صَغِيرِي.
dialogue.en: Whoever works hard finds success, my dear.

dialogue.ar: إِذَنْ سَأَفْعَلُ مَا يَلْزَمُ لِلنَّجَاحِ.
dialogue.en: Then I'll do whatever it takes to succeed.

b1-comparative.title: The Comparative: أفعل من
b1-comparative.explanation: Arabic makes almost any comparative from a triliteral root using the pattern أفعل — كبير ('big') becomes أكبر ('bigger'), and من means 'than': البيتُ أكبرُ من الشقةِ ('the house is bigger than the apartment'). One pattern, أفعل, covers what English needs '-er' and 'more' for.
b1-comparative.fact: This same أفعل pattern also produces most color and physical-defect adjectives — أحمر (red), أزرق (blue), أعمى (blind) — which is why they behave irregularly in other grammatical contexts too. The comparative and 'color/defect' patterns are historically the same mold.

dialogue.ar: أَيُّ السَّيَّارَتَيْنِ أَسْرَعُ؟
dialogue.en: Which of the two cars is faster?

dialogue.ar: هَذِهِ أَسْرَعُ مِنْ تِلْكَ، لَكِنَّهَا أَغْلَى أَيْضًا.
dialogue.en: This one is faster than that one, but it's also more expensive.

b1-superlative.title: The Superlative: الأفعل
b1-superlative.pattern: الأفعل + (iḍāfa with a definite plural, or في + place)
b1-superlative.explanation: Add ال to the same أفعل pattern and drop من, and the comparative becomes a superlative: الأكبر ('the biggest'). It's typically followed by في ('in') plus a place, or by iḍāfa with a definite plural: أكبرُ مدينةٍ (iḍāfa, indefinite singular) vs المدينةُ الأكبرُ (adjective agreement) — both exist, with slightly different emphasis.
b1-superlative.fact: Because أفعل is one mold used for both comparative and superlative, context (the presence of من vs الـ) is what tells you which one you're looking at — the word أكبر by itself is ambiguous between 'bigger' and (with ال) 'biggest' until you see how it's used in the sentence.

dialogue.ar: هَذَا أَكْبَرُ مَتْحَفٍ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ.
dialogue.en: This is the biggest museum in the city.

dialogue.ar: وَمَا هُوَ الْأَشْهَرُ بَيْنَ كُلِّ الْمَعَالِمِ هُنَا؟
dialogue.en: And what is the most famous among all the sites here?

b2-equality.title: Equality and Proportion
b2-equality.pattern: مثل / كـ = like, as · ليس بـ = not as · كلما...كلما = the more...the more
b2-equality.explanation: To say two things are equal rather than one being 'more', Arabic drops أفعل entirely and uses مثل or the prefix كـ ('like'): هذا البيتُ مثلَ ذلك البيتِ في الحجمِ ('this house is like that house in size'). كلما doubled up (كلما...كلما) builds proportional sentences: كلما اقتربنا زاد الازدحامُ ('the closer we got, the more the traffic increased').
b2-equality.fact: The كلما...كلما construction is grammatically a pair of conditional clauses in disguise — each كلما clause technically means 'every time that...', so the sentence is literally 'every time we got closer, every time the traffic increased', which English smooths into a single 'the more... the more' pattern.

dialogue.ar: هَذَا الْمَطْعَمُ لَيْسَ بِجَوْدَةِ الْمَطْعَمِ الْآخَرِ.
dialogue.en: This restaurant isn't as good as the other one.

dialogue.ar: أُوَافِقُكِ. وَكُلَّمَا زَادَ السِّعْرُ قَلَّتِ الْجَوْدَةُ هُنَا!
dialogue.en: I agree. And the higher the price, the lower the quality here!

b2-real-conditional.title: Real Conditionals: إذا
b2-real-conditional.pattern: إذا + past-form verb (present meaning), + present/imperative/future result
b2-real-conditional.explanation: For conditions that are real or likely — 'if you study, you'll pass' — Arabic uses إذا followed by a verb in the past form even though the meaning is present or future: إذا درستَ ستنجحُ (literally 'if you-studied [i.e. if you study], you will succeed'). This is just a grammatical convention, not a real past tense.
b2-real-conditional.fact: إن is إذا's stricter sibling, used for conditions that are less certain or more hypothetical, and it triggers the jussive mood rather than the past form. In everyday MSA, إذا has become the default for almost all real conditionals, while إن survives mostly in more formal or literary registers.

dialogue.ar: إِذَا اجْتَهَدْتَ فِي دِرَاسَتِكَ، سَأُكَافِئُكَ.
dialogue.en: If you work hard in your studies, I'll reward you.

dialogue.ar: حَقًّا؟ إِذًا سَأَدْرُسُ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ!
dialogue.en: Really? Then I'll study every day!

c1-hypothetical-conditional.title: Counterfactual لو
c1-hypothetical-conditional.pattern: لو + past verb, + لَ + past verb (result)
c1-hypothetical-conditional.explanation: لو is reserved for conditions that are contrary to fact — things that didn't happen or can't happen: لو كنتُ غنيًّا لسافرتُ ('if I were rich, I would travel' — but I'm not). The result clause is often marked with a prefixed لَ, which signals 'this is the hypothetical consequence' the way English 'would have' does.
c1-hypothetical-conditional.fact: Because لو always implies the condition is false, لو كنتَ صادقًا لصدّقتُكَ ('if you were honest, I'd believe you') carries a pointed implication the listener can't miss: you're not being honest. Arabic speakers use this to deliver criticism indirectly and quite sharply.

dialogue.ar: لَوْ عَرَفْتُ الْحَقِيقَةَ مِن الْبِدَايَةِ، لَتَصَرَّفْتُ بِشَكْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ.
dialogue.en: If I had known the truth from the start, I would have acted differently.

dialogue.ar: لَوْلَا صَمْتُكَ، لَمَا وَصَلْنَا إِلَى هَذِهِ الْمُشْكِلَةِ.
dialogue.en: Were it not for your silence, we wouldn't have reached this problem.

c1-temporal-causal.title: Temporal & Causal Connectives
c1-temporal-causal.pattern: عندما / متى ما / أينما = when / whenever / wherever · منذ / إذ = since
c1-temporal-causal.explanation: عندما introduces a specific 'when' clause and takes an ordinary verb, while متى ما and أينما ('whenever', 'wherever') add ما to make the time or place indefinite and typically trigger the jussive. منذ ('since') marks a starting point in time; إذ can mean 'since/given that' in a causal sense, or 'when' in narration.
c1-temporal-causal.fact: إذ has a second life as a purely stylistic connector in formal narration, roughly meaning 'at which point' — you'll see it constantly in news Arabic and historical writing, stitching together events without adding much new meaning of its own, similar to how English 'whereupon' functions in older prose.

dialogue.ar: عِنْدَمَا وَصَلْنَا، كَانَ الْجَمِيعُ يَنْتَظِرُ.
dialogue.en: When we arrived, everyone was waiting.

dialogue.ar: وَمُنْذُ ذَلِكَ الْيَوْمِ، لَمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ شَيْءٌ؟
dialogue.en: And since that day, nothing has changed?

c1-concessive-exceptive.title: Concessive & Exceptive Connectives
c1-concessive-exceptive.pattern: لولا + noun = were it not for · مهما / كيفما = however/whatever · حتى لو = even if · إلا إذا = unless
c1-concessive-exceptive.explanation: لولا is followed directly by a noun (not a verb) and means 'were it not for': لولا مساعدتُك, لما نجحتُ ('were it not for your help, I wouldn't have succeeded'). مهما and كيفما add concession — 'no matter what/how' — and trigger the jussive. حتى لو ('even if') and إلا إذا ('unless') both qualify a conditional from the opposite direction: one grants the point anyway, the other excludes it.
c1-concessive-exceptive.fact: لولا is grammatically unusual: almost every other Arabic particle that starts a sentence is followed by a verb, but لولا specifically demands a noun (or a pronoun suffix acting like one) — لولاك ('were it not for you') packs an entire conditional clause into a single word.

dialogue.ar: لَوْلَا صَبْرُ أَهْلِكَ، لَمَا وَصَلْتَ إِلَى هُنَا.
dialogue.en: Were it not for your family's patience, you wouldn't have gotten here.

dialogue.ar: أَعْرِفُ. وَمَهْمَا حَدَثَ، لَنْ أُخَيِّبَ ظَنَّهُمْ.
dialogue.en: I know. And whatever happens, I won't let them down.

c1-verb-derivation.title: Verb Derivation: The Ten Forms (الأوزان)
c1-verb-derivation.pattern: Form I فَعَلَ (base) → Form II فَعَّلَ (causative/intensive) → Form IV أَفْعَلَ (causative) → Form VIII اِفْتَعَلَ (reflexive/participatory) → Form X اِسْتَفْعَلَ (seeking/requesting)
c1-verb-derivation.explanation: Every Arabic root can, in principle, be poured into any of ten verb 'molds' (أوزان), each adding a predictable shift in meaning to the same three consonants. دَرَسَ (to study, Form I) becomes دَرَّسَ (to teach, Form II — causing someone else to study) and دَارَسَ (to study with someone, Form III). فَهِمَ (to understand) becomes أَفْهَمَ (to explain/clarify, Form IV) and اسْتَفْهَمَ (to inquire, Form X — 'to seek understanding').
c1-verb-derivation.fact: This derivational system means a fluent reader who's never seen a specific word before can often guess its meaning just from its shape and a known root — recognizing a Form X verb signals 'seeking/requesting X' even for an unfamiliar root, the way an English speaker can guess that 'defenestrate' involves a window without ever having heard it before.

dialogue.ar: مَا الْفَرْقُ بَيْنَ عَلِمَ وَعَلَّمَ؟
dialogue.en: What's the difference between 'alima' and 'allama'?

dialogue.ar: عَلِمَ يَعْنِي 'عَرَفَ'، أَمَّا عَلَّمَ فَتَعْنِي 'جَعَلَ غَيْرَهُ يَعْلَمُ'.
dialogue.en: 'Alima' means 'knew', while 'allama' means 'made someone else know' — 'taught'.

c1-idafa-advanced.title: Institutional Iḍāfa Chains
c1-idafa-advanced.pattern: noun + noun + noun (each definite by the last) = formal titles and institution names
c1-idafa-advanced.explanation: At an advanced level, iḍāfa chains stack to name real institutions and titles precisely: وزارةُ الشؤونِ الخارجيةِ ('the Ministry of Foreign Affairs') or رئيسُ مجلسِ إدارةِ الشركةِ ('the chairman of the company's board of directors'). Every noun in the chain except the very last stays free of ال and tanwīn — only the final noun carries the definiteness that flows back through the whole chain.
c1-idafa-advanced.fact: News Arabic runs almost entirely on these long iḍāfa chains — a single sentence in a political report might contain a five- or six-noun chain naming an exact office or committee, which is part of why formal Arabic news initially reads as denser than conversational speech: the grammar is doing more work per sentence.

dialogue.ar: أَعْلَنَتْ وِزَارَةُ الشُّؤُونِ الْخَارِجِيَّةِ عَنِ اجْتِمَاعٍ طَارِئٍ.
dialogue.en: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs announced an emergency meeting.

dialogue.ar: وَمَنْ هُوَ رَئِيسُ الْوَفْدِ الْمُشَارِكِ فِيهِ؟
dialogue.en: And who is the head of the delegation taking part in it?

c1-subjunctive.title: The Subjunctive Mood (المنصوب)
c1-subjunctive.pattern: أنْ / لن / كي / حتى + subjunctive verb (verb ends in ـَ instead of ـُ)
c1-subjunctive.explanation: A handful of particles — أن (that/to), لن (will not), كي (in order to), حتى (so that) — force the following present-tense verb into the subjunctive mood, marked by a fatḥa ending instead of the default ḍamma: يذهبُ (he goes) becomes أن يذهبَ (that he go / to go) after one of these particles.
c1-subjunctive.fact: This is grammatically the same mechanism as the English subjunctive fossil in 'I insist that he go' (not 'goes') — both languages mark a shift from stating a fact to expressing intention, possibility, or purpose with a special verb form, even though English mostly lost this distinction outside of a few formal expressions.

dialogue.ar: أُرِيدُ أَنْ تُنْهِيَ هَذَا التَّقْرِيرَ الْيَوْمَ.
dialogue.en: I want you to finish this report today.

dialogue.ar: سَأَعْمَلُ بِجِدٍّ حَتَّى أُنْهِيَهُ فِي الْوَقْتِ الْمُحَدَّدِ.
dialogue.en: I'll work hard so that I finish it on time.

c1-media-register.title: Media & Political Register
c1-media-register.pattern: صرّح بأنّ / نفى / نددّ بـ / أعلن عن — reporting verbs + their fixed prepositions
c1-media-register.explanation: News Arabic leans on a small set of reporting verbs, each with a preposition it always pairs with: صرّح بأنّ ('stated that'), نفى ('denied' — takes a direct object, no preposition), ندّد بـ ('condemned' — literally 'raised an alarm at'), أعلن عن ('announced'). Learning the verb-plus-preposition pair as a single unit is far more useful than learning either piece alone.
c1-media-register.fact: Much of this register descends from the more ornate style of classical Arabic oratory and diplomatic correspondence — the same tradition that produced elaborate historical chronicles — filtered down through 20th-century pan-Arab broadcasting into the fairly standardized 'news voice' heard across the Arabic-speaking world today, regardless of the reporter's home dialect.

dialogue.ar: صَرَّحَ الْمُتَحَدِّثُ بِأَنَّ الْمُفَاوَضَاتِ مُسْتَمِرَّةٌ.
dialogue.en: The spokesperson stated that negotiations are ongoing.

dialogue.ar: وَهَلْ نَفَتِ الْحُكُومَةُ التَّقَارِيرَ الْأَخِيرَةَ؟
dialogue.en: And did the government deny the recent reports?

c1-proverbs.title: Proverbs & Fixed Expressions
c1-proverbs.pattern: compact, often verb-first or noun-first phrasing with no filler words
c1-proverbs.explanation: Arabic proverbs (أمثال) compress a full observation into the fewest possible words, often dropping anything grammatically optional: الصبر مفتاح الفرج ('patience is the key to relief') is a plain nominal sentence with zero extra words. Many use من ('whoever') or parallel structure (في X ... وفي Y) for rhythm and memorability.
c1-proverbs.fact: Some of the most common Arabic proverbs used in everyday Modern Standard Arabic today trace back over a thousand years to pre-Islamic oral poetry and early Islamic literature — العصفور في اليد خير من عشرة على الشجرة and its many regional cousins appear, in variant forms, across nearly every language in the region, a reminder of just how much shared cultural material moved along old trade and pilgrimage routes.

dialogue.ar: تَذَكَّرْ: مَنْ جَدَّ وَجَدَ.
dialogue.en: Remember: whoever strives, finds success.

dialogue.ar: وَأَنْتَ عَلَّمْتَنِي أَيْضًا: فِي التَّأَنِّي السَّلَامَةُ.
dialogue.en: And you also taught me: in patience there is safety.

b2-passive-basic.title: The Passive Voice (المبني للمجهول)
b2-passive-basic.pattern: فُعِلَ (past passive) · يُفْعَلُ (present passive) — no agent needed
b2-passive-basic.explanation: The passive voice shifts the object of an action into subject position and removes the doer entirely: كتب الرجلُ الكتابَ ('the man wrote the book') becomes كُتِبَ الكتابُ ('the book was written'). Arabic marks this purely through internal vowel change — كَتَبَ becomes كُتِبَ in the past, يَكْتُبُ becomes يُكْتَبُ in the present — with no extra word needed at all.
b2-passive-basic.fact: Because the passive is built entirely from internal vowels that are usually left unwritten in normal Arabic text, a sentence like كتب الكتاب is genuinely ambiguous on the page between 'he wrote the book' and 'the book was written' — context, or the presence of a following subject, is what tells a reader which one is meant.

dialogue.ar: متى أُسِّسَت هذه الجريدةُ؟
dialogue.en: When was this newspaper founded?

dialogue.ar: أُسِّسَت منذ خمسين عامًا، وتُطبَعُ نسخةٌ جديدةٌ منها كلَّ صباحٍ.
dialogue.en: It was founded fifty years ago, and a new copy of it is printed every morning.

c1-passive-agent.title: Passive with an Agent — and Fourteen Centuries of History
c1-passive-agent.pattern: فُعِلَ + بواسطة / مِن قِبَل / على يدِ + agent = 'was done by...'
c1-passive-agent.explanation: Even though the whole point of the passive is usually to drop the doer, Arabic can still name one when needed, using بواسطة, من قِبَل, or على يدِ ('at the hand of') before the agent: أُلِّفَ هذا الكتابُ على يدِ عالمٍ كبيرٍ ('this book was authored by a great scholar'). This construction is the backbone of how Arabic narrates history — the actor is added back in only when it's the interesting part of the sentence.
c1-passive-agent.fact: Classical and Modern Standard Arabic historiography leans on the passive far more than English does, in part because it lets a sentence foreground the event itself — a conquest, a founding, a translation movement — before optionally naming who was responsible, which is exactly the rhetorical shape of most Arabic chronicles from the eighth century onward.

dialogue.ar: على يدِ مَن تُرجِمَت هذه الكتبُ اليونانيةُ؟
dialogue.en: By whose hand were these Greek books translated?

dialogue.ar: تُرجِمَت بواسطة علماءَ في بيتِ الحكمةِ في بغدادَ.
dialogue.en: They were translated by scholars at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad.

c1-passive-register.title: The Passive Across Registers
c1-passive-register.pattern: News: فُعِلَ (formal, agentless) · Routine: يُفْعَلُ (present, habitual)
c1-passive-register.explanation: The same passive mechanics serve two very different registers. News Arabic uses the past passive to report events with a detached, official tone — أُعلِنَ عن اكتشافٍ علميٍّ جديدٍ ('a new scientific discovery was announced'). Everyday Arabic uses the present passive for routines and customs performed by no one in particular — تُقدَّمُ القهوةُ العربيةُ في كلِّ مناسبةٍ ('Arabic coffee is served on every occasion').
c1-passive-register.fact: That second, domestic use of the passive is where a lot of Arabic hospitality culture gets encoded directly into grammar — describing coffee, dates, or a meal as something that 'is served' (rather than 'someone serves it') frames hospitality as an expected, almost automatic feature of the occasion itself, especially around Ramadan and Eid.

dialogue.ar: رائحةٌ رائعةٌ! ماذا يُطبَخُ اليومَ؟
dialogue.en: What a wonderful smell! What's being cooked today?

dialogue.ar: تُخبَزُ الحلوياتُ استعدادًا للعيدِ. تفضَّل، ستُقدَّمُ القهوةُ حالًا.
dialogue.en: Sweets are being baked in preparation for Eid. Please, coffee will be served shortly.

a2-imperative.title: The Imperative (الأمر)
a2-imperative.pattern: اُ + jussive stem (Form I) · a dedicated form per person: أنتَ / أنتِ / أنتم
a2-imperative.explanation: Arabic commands drop the subject entirely and reshape the verb itself — اكتبْ ('write!') is a complete sentence. The form changes with who you're addressing: اكتبْ (to a man), اكتبي (to a woman, adding ي), اكتبوا (to a group, adding وا). Negative commands switch to لا + jussive instead: لا تكتبْ ('don't write').
a2-imperative.fact: The imperative is built from the same jussive mood used after لم and لا — grammatically, 'go!' and 'did not go' share a verb base. Arabic reuses one mood for both blunt commands and negated past actions, which is a reminder that Arabic moods track grammatical function, not English-style tense categories.

dialogue.ar: اغسلْ يديك قبل الطعام يا ولدي.
dialogue.en: Wash your hands before eating, son.

dialogue.ar: حسنًا يا أمي، سأفعل ذلك حالًا.
dialogue.en: Okay, mom, I'll do that right away.

a2-plurals.title: Plurals: Sound and Broken (الجمع)
a2-plurals.pattern: Sound: -ون/-ين (m.), -ات (f.) · Broken: internal vowel-pattern change (كتاب → كتب)
a2-plurals.explanation: Arabic has two plural systems running side by side. Sound plurals just add an ending: معلم → معلمون. Broken plurals reshape the word's internal vowels into a new pattern with no predictable rule from the singular — كتاب becomes كتب, رجل becomes رجال. Most common nouns take broken plurals, which is why they simply have to be memorized word by word.
a2-plurals.fact: Arabic dictionaries list a noun's broken plural right next to its singular, precisely because you cannot reliably derive one from the other — a genuinely fluent vocabulary means learning roughly two forms per common noun, not one.

dialogue.ar: كم كتابًا قرأتم هذا الفصل؟
dialogue.en: How many books did you all read this semester?

dialogue.ar: قرأنا خمسة كتب، وكلها كانت ممتعة.
dialogue.en: We read five books, and they were all enjoyable.

a2-dual.title: The Dual Form (المثنى)
a2-dual.pattern: noun + ان (subject) / ين (object) — exactly two of something
a2-dual.explanation: Where English just says 'books' for two or more, Arabic has a separate form used only for exactly two: كتابان ('two books'), يَدَان ('two hands'). Verbs and adjectives describing two people or things take their own matching dual endings too — الطالبان يدرسان ('the two students [both] study').
a2-dual.fact: Classical Arabic, along with a handful of other ancient languages like Sanskrit and Old Greek, kept this three-way singular/dual/plural split that most of today's languages have lost — modern Arabic is one of the few living languages where 'two' still gets its own dedicated grammar, not just a number in front of the plural.

dialogue.ar: عندي تذكرتان لحفلٍ موسيقيٍّ الليلة.
dialogue.en: I have two tickets for a concert tonight.

dialogue.ar: رائع! سنقضي أمسيتين ممتعتين هذا الأسبوع إذن.
dialogue.en: Wonderful! We'll spend two enjoyable evenings this week then.

a2-kaana-sisters.title: Kāna and Her Sisters (كان وأخواتها)
a2-kaana-sisters.pattern: كان / أصبح / ظل / ما زال + noun (nominative) + adjective (accusative)
a2-kaana-sisters.explanation: This family of verbs plugs a verb into an otherwise verb-less nominal sentence, and each one adds its own flavor: كان marks simple past ('was'), أصبح/صار mark change ('became'), ظل/ما زال mark continuation ('remained/is still'). Whichever one is used, the adjective that follows switches from nominative to accusative case.
a2-kaana-sisters.fact: Arabic grammarians count roughly a dozen of these 'كان-sisters,' each encoding a subtly different shade of becoming, remaining, or ceasing to be — a level of grammatical precision around simple existence and change that English handles with far blunter tools like 'was' and 'became.'

dialogue.ar: أصبح الحي أكثر هدوءًا بعد إغلاق الشارع أمام السيارات.
dialogue.en: The neighborhood became quieter after the street was closed to cars.

dialogue.ar: صحيح، وما زلنا نستمتع بهذا الهدوء حتى الآن.
dialogue.en: True, and we're still enjoying this quiet even now.

a2-inna-sisters.title: Inna and Her Sisters (إن وأخواتها)
a2-inna-sisters.pattern: إنّ / أنّ / لكنّ / ليت / لعلّ + noun (accusative) + predicate
a2-inna-sisters.explanation: This second family works the opposite way from كان: instead of a verb, they're particles that push the following noun into the accusative case. Each carries its own meaning — إنّ/أنّ add emphasis or introduce a reported clause ('that...'), لكنّ contrasts ('but'), ليت wishes ('if only'), لعلّ hopes ('hopefully').
a2-inna-sisters.fact: أنّ is one of the most-used words in all of formal Arabic — nearly every reported statement, belief, or piece of news in the language ('I heard that...', 'I think that...') runs through it, which is why native speakers develop an instinct for accusative case on the very next noun almost automatically.

dialogue.ar: ليتك أخبرتني بالمشكلة قبل أن تتفاقم.
dialogue.en: I wish you had told me about the problem before it got worse.

dialogue.ar: أعرف، لكنّني ظننت أنني أستطيع حلها بنفسي.
dialogue.en: I know, but I thought I could solve it myself.

b1-prep-verbs.title: Verbs and Their Prepositions
b1-prep-verbs.pattern: verb + fixed preposition (فكّر في، اعتمد على، بحث عن...)
b1-prep-verbs.explanation: Many Arabic verbs are inseparable from a specific preposition, and changing it changes the meaning: بحث عن ('searched for'), بحث في ('researched/investigated'). These pairings don't translate word-for-word from English prepositions, so they're best learned as a single unit — verb-plus-preposition — rather than two separate pieces.
b1-prep-verbs.fact: This is exactly the same phenomenon as English phrasal verbs (look 'for' vs. look 'after' vs. look 'into') — every language seems to need some way to fine-tune a verb's meaning with a small attached word, and Arabic and English just solved the same problem independently, with different pairings.

dialogue.ar: هل بحثتَ عن حلٍّ لهذه المشكلة؟
dialogue.en: Did you search for a solution to this problem?

dialogue.ar: نعم، واعتمدتُ على نصيحة مديري في النهاية.
dialogue.en: Yes, and I relied on my manager's advice in the end.

b1-possessive-suffixes.title: Possessive Suffixes (ضمائر الملكية المتصلة)
b1-possessive-suffixes.pattern: noun + ي / كَ / كِ / هُ / ها / نا / كم / هم = my/your/his/her/our/your(pl)/their
b1-possessive-suffixes.explanation: Instead of a separate possessive word like English 'my,' Arabic attaches a small suffix directly onto the noun: كتابي ('my book'), كتابُكَ ('your book'), كتابُها ('her book'). These same suffixes double as object pronouns on verbs (رأيتُهُ, 'I saw him'), so learning this one set of endings covers two grammatical jobs at once.
b1-possessive-suffixes.fact: Because the suffix attaches to the noun itself, iḍāfa and possessive suffixes can't be mixed on the same noun — you can say بيتُ صديقي ('my friend's house') but never add ال or another possessive marker on top, which is why Arabic 'the my book' constructions that trip up beginners in other languages simply can't happen here.

dialogue.ar: هل أحضرتَ كتابك ودفترك اليوم؟
dialogue.en: Did you bring your book and your notebook today?

dialogue.ar: أحضرتُ كتابي، لكنني نسيتُ دفتري في البيت.
dialogue.en: I brought my book, but I forgot my notebook at home.

b1-conjunctions.title: Conjunctions (حروف العطف)
b1-conjunctions.pattern: و (and) · فـ (then, immediately) · ثم (then, later) · أو (or) · لكن (but) · بل (rather)
b1-conjunctions.explanation: Arabic distinguishes between two kinds of 'and then': فـ links two things that happen in tight sequence or as a direct consequence, while ثم marks a bigger gap in time between them. بل doesn't just contrast like لكن — it actively corrects the previous statement, as in 'not lazy, بل sick.'
b1-conjunctions.fact: فـ is technically a prefix, not a standalone word — it attaches directly to whatever follows it (فخرج, 'and-then-he-left'), which is part of why fluent Arabic prose often reads as a chain of tightly fused clauses rather than the more separated sentences typical of English writing.

dialogue.ar: درستُ بجدٍّ فنجحتُ في الامتحان بتفوقٍ.
dialogue.en: I studied hard, and so I passed the exam with distinction.

dialogue.ar: لم أدرس كفاية، لكنني نجحتُ أيضًا بفضل الله.
dialogue.en: I didn't study enough, but I passed too, thank God.

b1-numbers-counted.title: Numbers 3-10 with Counted Nouns (العدد والمعدود)
b1-numbers-counted.pattern: 3-10 + plural noun, with reversed gender agreement (gender polarity)
b1-numbers-counted.explanation: Numbers 3 through 10 follow a rule that surprises most learners: the number takes the opposite gender of the noun it counts. ثلاثة كتبٍ ('three books') pairs the masculine-looking ثلاثة with the masculine كتب, while ثلاثُ بناتٍ ('three girls') pairs the feminine ثلاث with the feminine بنات — masculine noun, feminine-marked number, and vice versa.
b1-numbers-counted.fact: This 'gender polarity' rule is one of the most distinctive features of Semitic-language numeral systems and has no parallel in English at all — it's consistently one of the last things even advanced learners get automatically right, since the instinct to match genders (rather than flip them) runs deep from other grammar patterns.

dialogue.ar: كم ولدًا وبنتًا في صفك هذا العام؟
dialogue.en: How many boys and girls are in your class this year?

dialogue.ar: عندنا سبعةُ أولادٍ وسبعُ بناتٍ بالضبط.
dialogue.en: We have exactly seven boys and seven girls.

b1-numbers-ordinals.title: Numbers 11-100 and Ordinals
b1-numbers-ordinals.pattern: الأول، الثاني، الثالث... = first, second, third (agree like adjectives)
b1-numbers-ordinals.explanation: Unlike cardinal numbers (three, four), ordinal numbers (third, fourth) behave like ordinary adjectives in Arabic — they follow the noun and agree with it in gender and definiteness: اليومُ الثالثُ ('the third day'), المرةُ الثالثةُ ('the third time', feminine because مرة is feminine).
b1-numbers-ordinals.fact: Arabic ordinals from 'first' through 'tenth' are each their own irregular word (أول، ثانٍ، ثالث...), but starting at eleventh, the ordinal is simply formed by treating the cardinal number as a description — يوم الحادي عشر, 'the eleventh day' — which mirrors how English also gets simpler and more mechanical past 'twentieth.'

dialogue.ar: هذه ثالث مرةٍ أزور فيها هذه المدينة.
dialogue.en: This is the third time I've visited this city.

dialogue.ar: وأنا زرتها للمرة الأولى فقط الأسبوع الماضي.
dialogue.en: And I only visited it for the first time last week.

b1-exclamations.title: Exclamations (التعجب)
b1-exclamations.pattern: ما أفعلَ + noun! (fixed pattern) · كم + clause! (flexible)
b1-exclamations.explanation: ما أفعل is a frozen grammatical mold — أفعل never changes form regardless of who or what you're exclaiming about: ما أجملَ هذا! ('how beautiful this is!') works exactly the same whether the subject is masculine, feminine, or plural. كم is more flexible and can precede a full clause for the same emphatic effect.
b1-exclamations.fact: The أفعل shape in ما أجمل is the exact same elative pattern used for comparatives and superlatives (أجمل also means 'more/most beautiful') — Arabic reuses one morphological mold for three different jobs: comparing, ranking as best, and exclaiming, and only word order and context tell you which one you're looking at.

dialogue.ar: ما أجمل هذا المنظر الطبيعي!
dialogue.en: How beautiful this natural view is!

dialogue.ar: كم أنا سعيدٌ لأنك أعجبت بهذا المكان!
dialogue.en: How happy I am that you liked this place!

b1-nisba.title: Nisba Adjectives (ياء النسبة)
b1-nisba.pattern: noun + يّ (masc.) / يّة (fem.) = 'relating to / from' that noun
b1-nisba.explanation: Adding يّ (with a shadda) onto almost any noun turns it into an adjective of belonging: مصر ('Egypt') becomes مصريّ ('Egyptian'), تاريخ ('history') becomes تاريخيّ ('historical'). It's one of the most productive patterns in the language — new nisba adjectives get coined constantly for nationalities, fields of study, and abstract relationships alike.
b1-nisba.fact: Nisba is where a huge share of English's own Arabic loanwords come from — 'algebra,' by way of الجبر, and countless place-derived adjectives in translated texts trace back to exactly this يّ suffix doing its job of turning a noun into 'belonging to X.'

dialogue.ar: هل هذا الأستاذ مصريٌّ أم لبنانيٌّ؟
dialogue.en: Is this professor Egyptian or Lebanese?

dialogue.ar: هو مصريٌّ، لكنه يدرّس مادةً تاريخيةً عن كل المنطقة.
dialogue.en: He's Egyptian, but he teaches a historical subject about the whole region.

b1-exception.title: Exception with إلا (الاستثناء)
b1-exception.explanation: إلا carves an exception out of a larger stated group: حضر الطلاب إلا واحدًا ('the students attended except one'). The excluded noun after إلا typically takes the accusative case when the sentence is affirmative, marking it clearly as the one item set apart from the rule.
b1-exception.fact: In a negative sentence, إلا flips into meaning 'only' rather than 'except': ما حضر إلا واحدٌ literally reads as 'none attended except one,' which functionally just means 'only one attended' — the same particle, one word, delivering two opposite-feeling but logically identical meanings depending on whether the sentence itself is already negated.

dialogue.ar: هل حضر كل الموظفين الاجتماع؟
dialogue.en: Did all the employees attend the meeting?

dialogue.ar: حضروا جميعًا إلا موظفًا واحدًا كان مريضًا.
dialogue.en: They all attended except one employee who was sick.

b2-masdar.title: Verbal Nouns (المصدر)
b2-masdar.pattern: قِرَاءَةٌ / فَهْمٌ / تَأَخُّرٌ — the action itself, used as a noun
b2-masdar.explanation: The maṣdar turns a verb into the noun of its own action: قرأ ('he read') gives قراءة ('reading'); فهم ('he understood') gives فهم ('understanding'). Once it's a noun, it behaves like any other noun — it can be a sentence's subject (قراءة الكتب تفيد, 'reading books is beneficial'), follow a preposition (بعد وصوله, 'after his arrival'), or intensify its own verb as the مفعول مطلق (رفضه رفضاً قاطعاً, 'he refused with a categorical refusal'). Each verb form (I–X) has its own maṣdar pattern, and form I maṣdars in particular have to be memorized per verb rather than derived by a single rule.
b2-masdar.fact: Because a maṣdar packages an entire action into a single noun, formal Arabic leans on it constantly to avoid subordinate clauses — where English needs 'after he arrived, ...', Arabic can just say بعد وصوله ('after his arrival'), which is why news Arabic and legal Arabic read as strings of noun phrases rather than chains of little clauses.

dialogue.ar: لماذا تُكْثِرُ الصُّحُفُ مِنِ اسْتِخْدَامِ الْمَصْدَرِ بَدَلًا مِنَ الْفِعْلِ؟
dialogue.en: Why do newspapers use the verbal noun so much instead of the verb?

dialogue.ar: لِأَنَّ الْمَصْدَرَ يُلَخِّصُ الْحَدَثَ فِي كَلِمَةٍ وَاحِدَةٍ، فَتُصْبِحُ الْجُمْلَةُ أَكْثَرَ إِيجَازًا وَرَسْمِيَّةً.
dialogue.en: Because the verbal noun sums up the whole event in one word, which makes the sentence more concise and formal.

b2-participles.title: Active and Passive Participles (اسم الفاعل واسم المفعول)
b2-participles.explanation: The active participle (ism al-fā'il) names the one doing the action — كاتب means 'writer/writing (one)', from كتب. The passive participle (ism al-maf'ūl) names the one the action happens to — مكتوب means 'written'. For form I verbs the patterns are fixed: فَاعِل for active, مَفْعُول for passive (كتب → كاتب / مكتوب; فهم → فاهم / مفهوم). Both function as adjectives or nouns and, crucially, can carry present or near-future meaning on their own, without any verb: أنا ذاهبٌ غدًا ('I am going tomorrow') uses no verb at all — ذاهب alone does the job.
b2-participles.fact: Because an active participle can stand in for a present-tense verb, MSA often prefers هو مسافرٌ الآن ('he is traveling now') over a verb-based construction — participles feel more like a snapshot of a state, while the verb يسافر feels more like an ongoing process, a nuance that doesn't map cleanly onto English's single present tense.

dialogue.ar: هَلْ أَنْتَ ذَاهِبٌ إِلَى الْمَطَارِ الْآنَ؟
dialogue.en: Are you going to the airport now?

dialogue.ar: نَعَمْ، وَالطَّرِيقُ مَغْلُوقٌ جُزْئِيًّا بِسَبَبِ الصِّيَانَةِ.
dialogue.en: Yes, and the road is partially closed because of maintenance.

b2-hal-tamyiz.title: Circumstantial and Specification Accusative (الحال والتمييز)
b2-hal-tamyiz.pattern: حَال: -ًا/-ةً describing a simultaneous state — تَمْيِيز: -ًا disambiguating a vague noun
b2-hal-tamyiz.explanation: الحال answers 'in what state?' at the moment of the main action — جاء مسرعًا ('he came hurriedly') tells you how he came, not what he did. التمييز answers 'specified in what respect?' after a noun that's otherwise ambiguous — عشرون طالبًا ('twenty students') needs طالبًا to say what's being counted, since عشرون alone is just a number. Both are marked accusative (with tanwīn -ًا in most cases) and both exist to resolve an ambiguity the main sentence leaves open: الحال resolves 'in what manner', التمييز resolves 'of what kind'.
b2-hal-tamyiz.fact: التمييز is exactly why Arabic numbers 11–99 are always followed by a singular indefinite accusative noun (خمسون كتابًا, 'fifty books') rather than a plural — the number is treated as inherently vague about what it's counting until the تمييز pins it down, so the counted noun is grammatically a specification, not a normal plural object.

dialogue.ar: كَمْ طَالِبًا فِي صَفِّكَ هَذَا الْعَامَ؟
dialogue.en: How many students are in your class this year?

dialogue.ar: خَمْسَةٌ وَثَلَاثُونَ طَالِبًا، وَجَاءَ أَغْلَبُهُمْ مُتَحَمِّسِينَ فِي الْيَوْمِ الْأَوَّلِ.
dialogue.en: Thirty-five students, and most of them came enthusiastic on the first day.

b2-purpose.title: Purpose Clauses (لكي، كي، حتى، لـ)
b2-purpose.pattern: لِـ / كَيْ / لِكَيْ / حَتَّى + subjunctive verb
b2-purpose.explanation: To say 'in order to', Arabic puts the subjunctive mood (منصوب) after a small set of particles: لـ (the most common and most formal), كي, لكي (a fusion of the two), and حتى when it means 'in order to' rather than 'until'. All four trigger the same subjunctive ending on the following verb — درس بجدٍّ لينجحَ ('he studied hard so that he would succeed') — and are broadly interchangeable, though لـ and حتى are more common in everyday register while لكي feels slightly more formal or written.
b2-purpose.fact: حتى is genuinely ambiguous out of context between 'until' (with an indicative verb) and 'in order to' (with a subjunctive one) — درس حتى ينجحَ can mean 'he studied in order to succeed', while درس حتى نجحَ (indicative) means 'he studied until he succeeded' — the mood marking on the verb, invisible in unvoweled text, is the only thing that distinguishes the two readings.

dialogue.ar: لِمَاذَا تَتَدَرَّبُ كُلَّ صَبَاحٍ قَبْلَ الْمُبَارَاةِ؟
dialogue.en: Why do you train every morning before the match?

dialogue.ar: أَتَدَرَّبُ لِكَيْ أَحْتَفِظَ بِلِيَاقَتِي وَأَتَجَنَّبَ الْإِصَابَاتِ.
dialogue.en: I train so that I maintain my fitness and avoid injuries.

b2-time-clauses.title: Advanced Time Clauses (حين، بينما، ريثما)
b2-time-clauses.explanation: Beyond the basic عندما ('when'), formal Arabic has a fuller toolkit for relating two events in time: حين (a more literary 'when'), بينما ('while', for two things happening simultaneously), and ريثما ('until, for as long as it takes', framing one action as bounded by another's duration). Unlike English, most of these can open the sentence and simply attach a full verbal clause without any linking word — حين وصل، كان الجميع قد غادروا ('when he arrived, everyone had already left').
b2-time-clauses.fact: ريثما is one of the few Arabic time connectors that inherently frames its clause as a *duration* rather than a *point* — انتظر ريثما ينتهي الاجتماع doesn't just mean 'wait when the meeting ends' but 'wait for exactly as long as the meeting takes', which is why it's common in formal instructions and legal texts where precise duration matters.

dialogue.ar: هَلْ أَنْتَظِرُ هُنَا رَيْثَمَا يَنْتَهِي الِاجْتِمَاعُ؟
dialogue.en: Should I wait here until the meeting ends?

dialogue.ar: نَعَمْ، وَبَيْنَمَا تَنْتَظِرِينَ يُمْكِنُكِ مُرَاجَعَةَ التَّقْرِيرِ.
dialogue.en: Yes, and while you wait you can review the report.

c1-emphasis.title: Emphasis Particles (إنّ، لقد، لام التوكيد)
c1-emphasis.pattern: إِنَّ + accusative subject — لَقَدْ + past verb — لَـ + imperfect/noun
c1-emphasis.explanation: Formal Arabic has a dedicated grammar of emphasis rather than relying on tone of voice. إنّ opens a sentence and puts its subject in the accusative, sharpening a plain statement into an assertion (الطقس جميل → إنّ الطقس جميلٌ). لقد precedes a past-tense verb to underline that something has definitely, completely happened (لقد وصلنا, 'we have indeed arrived'). The لام المزحلقة ('sliding lām') attaches directly to a word — often after إنّ — to add a second layer of emphasis (إنّه لَجميلٌ, 'it is indeed beautiful'). These can stack, and formal registers (speeches, editorials, religious texts) use them far more densely than everyday speech.
c1-emphasis.fact: Stacking emphasis devices is a real stylistic register, not redundancy — a sentence like إنّه لَقَدْ نجحَ نجاحًا باهرًا piles up إنّ, the لام, and a مفعول مطلق all at once, and rhetoric handbooks treat the number of emphasis markers a writer uses as a deliberate dial for how forcefully a claim is being made.

dialogue.ar: إِنَّ هَذَا الْإِنْجَازَ لَثَمَرَةُ سَنَوَاتٍ مِنَ الْجُهْدِ الْمُشْتَرَكِ.
dialogue.en: This achievement is indeed the fruit of years of joint effort.

dialogue.ar: لَقَدْ لَمَسْنَا جَمِيعًا حَجْمَ هَذَا الْجُهْدِ عَنْ قُرْبٍ.
dialogue.en: We have indeed all seen up close the scale of this effort.

c1-reported-speech.title: Reported Speech (الكلام المنقول)
c1-reported-speech.explanation: Arabic has no tense-backshifting the way English does ('he said he *was* coming') — reported speech simply nests the original statement, unchanged in tense, after a reporting verb and إنّ/أنّ: قال إنه سيحضر غدًا reports someone's exact words 'I will attend tomorrow' without shifting سيحضر into a past form. Journalistic Arabic has a graded set of reporting verbs beyond قال ('said'): صرّح بـ ('stated publicly'), أفاد ('reported/indicated'), أكد ('affirmed'), زعم ('claimed', implying doubt), and أشار إلى ('indicated'), each signaling the reporter's stance toward the reliability of the quote.
c1-reported-speech.fact: The choice of reporting verb in Arabic news writing is a subtle editorial signal — زعم ('claimed') versus أكد ('affirmed') carry the same reported content but opposite implications about whether the outlet believes the speaker, which is why press-freedom and media-literacy courses in Arabic specifically teach students to notice which verb a headline uses.

dialogue.ar: مَاذَا صَرَّحَ الْوَزِيرُ بِخُصُوصِ الْأَزْمَةِ؟
dialogue.en: What did the minister state regarding the crisis?

dialogue.ar: أَفَادَ بِأَنَّ الْحُكُومَةَ سَتَتَّخِذُ إِجْرَاءَاتٍ عَاجِلَةً هَذَا الْأُسْبُوعَ.
dialogue.en: He indicated that the government would take urgent measures this week.

c1-legal-register.title: Legal and Contractual Register
c1-legal-register.pattern: يَلْتَزِمُ الطَّرَفُ الْأَوَّلُ بِـ ... / بِمُوجَبِ الْمَادَّةِ ...
c1-legal-register.explanation: Legal Arabic favors dense noun phrases and passive constructions over everyday verb-first sentences, to keep responsibility and obligation unambiguous: الطرف الأول ('the first party'), بموجب ('pursuant to'), يلتزم بـ ('is obligated to'), وبناءً على ('and based on') are all stock phrases that recur across contracts, laws, and rulings. Passive verbs (تُعَدُّ هذه الوثيقة ملزمةً, 'this document is deemed binding') are preferred precisely because they avoid naming who is asserting something, letting the text itself carry the authority.
c1-legal-register.fact: Legal Arabic keeps a noticeably higher density of case-ending vowels (i'rāb) marked or implied than everyday MSA, because a single missing case ending on a word like الطرفِ can genuinely shift who owes what to whom — which is why formal contracts are one of the few contexts where fully vowel-marked Arabic still matters practically, not just aesthetically.

dialogue.ar: بِمُوجَبِ الْمَادَّةِ الْخَامِسَةِ، يَلْتَزِمُ الطَّرَفُ الثَّانِي بِتَسْدِيدِ الْمَبْلَغِ خِلَالَ شَهْرٍ.
dialogue.en: Pursuant to Article Five, the second party is obligated to pay the amount within a month.

dialogue.ar: وَمَاذَا يَحْدُثُ إِذَا لَمْ يُسَدَّدِ الْمَبْلَغُ فِي الْمَوْعِدِ الْمُحَدَّدِ؟
dialogue.en: And what happens if the amount is not paid by the specified date?

c1-academic-register.title: Academic and Scientific Register
c1-academic-register.pattern: تُشِيرُ الدِّرَاسَةُ إِلَى أَنَّ ... / تَهْدُفُ هَذِهِ الْوَرَقَةُ إِلَى ...
c1-academic-register.explanation: Academic Arabic writes findings as impersonal statements about the research itself rather than about the researcher: تُشِير الدراسة إلى أنّ ('the study indicates that'), تَبَيَّنَ أنّ ('it became evident that'), and خَلُصَ الباحثون إلى ('the researchers concluded'). Hedging language is standard practice, not weakness — قد تُشير النتائج إلى ('the results may indicate') is preferred over blunt claims, and comparative/contrastive connectors (في حين أنّ, 'whereas'; على النقيض من ذلك, 'in contrast') do heavy lifting to structure an argument across long paragraphs.
c1-academic-register.fact: Modern Arabic academic writing borrows a large share of its connector vocabulary (بالتالي, 'consequently'; وبناءً عليه, 'and accordingly') directly from formal written prose rather than spoken Arabic, so students are often explicitly taught these as a separate register in university writing courses, distinct from the Arabic they grew up speaking.

dialogue.ar: تُشِيرُ نَتَائِجُ الدِّرَاسَةِ إِلَى أَنَّ الْعَامِلَ الِاجْتِمَاعِيَّ أَكْثَرُ تَأْثِيرًا مِمَّا كَانَ مُتَوَقَّعًا.
dialogue.en: The study's results indicate that the social factor is more influential than had been expected.

dialogue.ar: وَبِنَاءً عَلَيْهِ، هَلْ تَقْتَرِحِينَ إِعَادَةَ صِيَاغَةِ الْفَرَضِيَّةِ الْأَصْلِيَّةِ؟
dialogue.en: Accordingly, do you suggest reformulating the original hypothesis?

c1-diplomatic-register.title: Diplomatic and International Relations Register
c1-diplomatic-register.pattern: أَعْرَبَ الْوَفْدُ عَنْ ... / دَعَا الْبَيَانُ الْمُشْتَرَكُ إِلَى ...
c1-diplomatic-register.explanation: Diplomatic Arabic favors carefully hedged, face-saving phrasing built around a stock set of verbal-noun constructions: أعرب عن قلقه ('expressed his concern', literally 'expressed of his concern'), دعا إلى ('called for'), شدّد على ('stressed'), رحّب بـ ('welcomed'). Ambiguity is often deliberate — بحث الطرفان سبل تعزيز التعاون ('the two sides discussed ways to enhance cooperation') commits to nothing specific, which is precisely the point in a joint statement meant to paper over disagreement.
c1-diplomatic-register.fact: Diplomatic communiqués are read almost like code by analysts: the specific verb chosen (رحّب بـ 'welcomed' vs. أحاط علمًا بـ 'took note of' vs. أدان 'condemned') signals a precise level of approval or disapproval on a fixed scale, so translators working in diplomacy are trained to never substitute a near-synonym, since swapping أحاط علماً for رحّب can misrepresent a government's actual position.

dialogue.ar: أَعْرَبَ الْوَفْدُ عَنْ تَقْدِيرِهِ لِجُهُودِ الْوِسَاطَةِ الَّتِي بُذِلَتْ خِلَالَ الْأَسَابِيعِ الْأَخِيرَةِ.
dialogue.en: The delegation expressed its appreciation for the mediation efforts made during the past weeks.

dialogue.ar: وَهَلْ دَعَا الْبَيَانُ الْمُشْتَرَكُ إِلَى اسْتِئْنَافِ الْمُفَاوَضَاتِ رَسْمِيًّا؟
dialogue.en: And did the joint statement call for formally resuming negotiations?
